author: @paulbrouyere1735
text: I appreciate you posted this video, but I do have some remarks to be considered. A round tube or guywire has 10 times more drag than an aerodynamic shaped profile of the same thickness. The reason why blades on large turbines are tilted is to slow down rotor speed until a complete stop during heavy storms. At the time I read many reports, if I remember well, the structure must be able to withstand a windspeed of 50m/s during 2seconds. That is a vast amount of energy. If I see how many tubes and nodes are involved in this structure, I wish the engineers the very best to try and prevent this from being blown over.

author: @alaingrignon
text: Rob, This concept is precisely what I'm trying to get going in Canada! I proposed a distributed electricity generation strategy (not just wind but all renewable). I developed it back in 2016 and just the other day I got a call to describe it in more detail as we start adapting to a new government term. :) There is no direct path to success when it comes to innovation, but I'll take what I can get.

author: @kilokilos
text: Take an oil drum, cut it into 5 equal blades along the vertical, bend the blades at 36' angle with 2/3 sticking out. Make two and weld them together and turn it horizontal on bearings. Turns from all wind directions plus it has a " sail" effect if the wind comes at an angle. Put two opposing cones in the middle to cover the hole when looking from the side Cheap and effective it produces talk instead of just speed.will know the actual output soon.

author: @dexterdixon2000
text: R.e. your point about large turbine tips reaching a speed limit - it's my understanding that the tip speed ratio is broadly similar for large and small machines - where did you get the fact that large ones reach a limit but small ones don't? Cheers.

replies.author: @nil5221
replies.text:
TSR isn't actually a "limit". It's an ideal range (6-8) that the turbine blade should be designed to operate within, for maximum power extraction from the wind. Too slow, and most of the wind passes through without any power being extracted. Too fast, and the turbine acts as a "wall" and the wind tries to go around it.
What Rob referred to is that "absolute" limit of tip speed, in a tangential direction. Not the ratio to wind speed. 
As turbine blades are made larger and larger, the danger is, when operating at ideal TSR, the tip speed approaches supersonic. We can't have that. So they have to be made to slow down, below ideal TSR, thus becoming inefficient, losing the benefit of having the longer blades in the first place.
So all the money involved in raising a massive turbine up high to utilise the higher wind speed. at some point becomes not worthwhile,

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry but you didn't listen to the video properly - I never talked about tip speed ratio - I talked about speed at the tip - it's not the same thing

replies.author: @dexterdixon2000
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering the speed at the tip is the same on big turbines as it is on small turbines. I did listen! I think the philosophy of redundancy is sound - just picking up on the point your making Ng at 3.50 about the limits of large machines. They ARE reaching the limit of the materials but not due to the speed at the tips - this is the same regardless of turbine size.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: re-read what SteveMac said - It may help you understand

replies.author: @dexterdixon2000
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering the way he describes tsr is indeed good - not missing the wind etc. So a small blade has an ideal tsr of 7, and a large blade also has an ideal tsr of 7. In a 10mph wind the tip of both turbines wants to go at 70mph. For the larger turbine this happens at a lower RPM which the gear box compensates for. What am I missing?

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
The 'i' in RAID sometimes stands for 'inexpensive.'  Key to a redundant wind generation RAIT system will be to make windcatcher system components very cheaply.  A lot of the ideas you've explored would be great for such a system. 
A note about wind turbines changing blades -- you are correct, that the real lifespan of a blade is about 10 years, failure or early replacements usually involves complete demolition and replacement of the facility.  All in all, this tends to validate your idea.  
"Dutch" technology for windmills was actually Indian or Arabian sailing boat technology, imported through Spain.  The Nederlands actually made the ideal best development and use of the technology, pumping out polders.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice points mate and thanks for the correction - I guess the tech used is even older than I thought - cheers

author: @totherarf
text:
There is an optimum size for wind turbines. To big and you run into the square verses the cube rule (mentioned in the video) but reducing the size too much and you run into problems of number of units to have a meaningful output!

The Wind Wall concept can increase the output due to the increased air pressure on the lead side (the wind is being obstructed by a wall of fans moving slower than the wind) and decreased pressure on the downwind side. 
One thing that jumps out at me is to plug the holes in the wall! Those gaps between adjacent fans will let wind through that could be used! Another thing is that the wall is not rotatable so will only be efficient with prevailing winds as found on the coast!
